Service Stored Procedures on PostgreSQL

Service stored procedures are supported for the PGS connector.

The stored procedure name used for execution by Uniface is derived from the schema name, as specified in the assignment file, and the Uniface operation name. When not specified, SchemaName defaults to "public".

Table 1. Component Name and Operation Name Specification
Literal Component Name Literal Operation Name Stored Procedure Name Example
Not specified Not specified SchemaName.UnifaceOperationName schema.sp1
Not specified Specified

LiteralOperationName 1

sp1

Specified Not specified LiteralComponentName.UnifaceOperationName

sp.SP1

Specified Specified LiteralComponentName.LiteralOperationName

sp.sp1

You can specify the SchemaName in the LiteralOperationName, for example: SchemaName.OperationName This SchemaName overrides the schema name specified in assignment file.

Parameter names are not used during the execution of stored procedures. However, their order (that is, their relative position) is used for mapping parameters. For more information, see Naming Specifications for Service Store Procedures.

Note: All names are case-sensitive.

Entity Parameters

The PGS connector does not support stored procedures with multiple entity parameters—a maximum of one entity parameter is allowed. This is due to a limitation in the current PostgreSQL release which prevents prepared statements from supporting multiple result sets.

When defining an operation with an entity parameter in a Uniface signature, you can define an entity parameter only as OUT or INOUT, but when creating the corresponding stored procedure, you must define the parameter as an OUTPUT string.

Data Conversions

The data types of operation parameters must closely match the stored procedure parameters. If necessary, explicit and implicit conversions are used to achieve an optimal mapping for all data types.

The supported parameter data type mappings for PostgreSQL are shown in the following table. (The data type mappings shown in the table apply to both basic parameters and entity parameters.)

Table 2. Uniface to PostgreSQL Parameter–Data Type Mappings

Uniface Parameter Data Types
PostgreSQL Data Types String Boolean Numeric Float (L)Date (L)Time (L)Datetime
char, character Y Y Y Y
varchar Y Y Y Y
character varying Y Y Y Y
decimal Y Y Y Y
smallint Y Y Y Y
bigint Y Y Y Y
integer Y Y Y Y
numeric Y Y Y Y
double precision Y Y Y
real Y Y Y
date Y Y Y Y
time without time zone Y Y Y
timestamp without time zone Y Y Y Y

NULL Support

Empty Uniface boolean data types are always mapped to FALSE. All other empty input string values are mapped to PostgreSQL values as NULL values. This is standard functionality.

Example: Service Stored Procedures

The following example shows a Service Stored Procedure with two basic parameters. It is executed with the following activate ProcScript statement:

activate "WORKTODO".ADDONE($in$, $out$)

The ADDONE operation has the following parameters:

Parameter name Type Data type Input/Output
P1 Basic String Input
P2 Basic String Output

The PostgreSQL script is as follows:

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION ADDONE(
 IN v1 integer,
 OUT v2 integer)
 RETURNS integer AS
$BODY$
begin
 v2 := v1 + 1;
end;
$BODY$
 LANGUAGE plpgsql;

Example: Service Stored Procedure with One Entity Parameter

The second example shows a Service Stored Procedure with one entity parameter. In this example, we assume a model M, and entity ENT001.

Table 3. ENT01 Entity Fields
Field Name Field Type Data Type
PK Numeric N20
F1 String C40

It is executed with the following activate ProcScript statement:

activate "WORKTODO".ENTITY_TEST("ENT001")

The ENT_TEST operation has the single output entity parameter shown in the table:

Parameter Name Type Data Type Input/Output Corresponding Model Name Entity Name
P1 Entity — Output M ENT001

The PostgreSQL script is as follows:

create or replace function ENTITY_TEST(v1 varchar(40))
returns setof TEST02
as
$$
RETURN QUERY
SELECT * FROM TEST02;
$$ 
language sql;

LikeDislike
